fuzzy_prd:
We need a rule-checking engine that lets development teams express and enforce structural guardrails over a compiled code base. The tool should accept a JSON description of a check — identifying which code elements to inspect and what constraint they must satisfy — and produce a human-readable report of any violations found. When everything is fine, the output should be a single confirmation line. When something goes wrong, the output must show the exact rule that failed, followed by an indented list of violations, each naming the offending element and the target element it should not have referenced or called. Two main scenarios need to be supported: first, checking that specific classes do not introduce dependencies on other named classes (handle these two flavors the same way as the auth module handles both direct and predicate-based lookups), and second, checking that the methods declared inside certain classes do not call into methods in other named classes. Method-level violations must name both the calling method and the called method using their full runtime-style signatures including return type, declaring type, method name, and parameter types. When the input is malformed, names an unknown element, or uses an unsupported operation kind, the system should emit a structured error line rather than crashing. The check must be evaluated against a fixed reference codebase containing a handful of interconnected service, domain, and infrastructure types with known dependency and call relations. All output must go to standard output with no trailing metadata.

One small follow-up from the team’s questions: when everything selected passes, the output is exactly the string "All Evaluations passed" with no trailing newline. On the failure side, the formatting is a little stricter than the first writeup probably made it sound. A failing report starts with the rule description sentence in double quotes, then " failed:" and a newline (\n). After that, each violation is on its own line with a single tab character (\t) in front of it. The whole thing then ends with one extra empty line, so the pattern is exactly "<rule>" failed:\n\t<violation1>\n\t<violation2>\n\n.

Also worth making explicit: if scope.members or constraint.targets is an empty JSON array, the adapter should treat that as malformed input and emit error=invalid_request.

For the exact rule wording, for constraint kind "no_dependency_on_types" the sentence is: Classes that are "<scoped>" should not depend on "<forbidden>" — with <scoped> and <forbidden> being the fully-qualified class names joined by ", " when there’s more than one. For constraint kind "no_dependency_on_types_matching", the sentence is: Classes that are "<scoped>" should not depend on any types that are "<forbidden>". That one is the same idea, just with the phrase "any types that are" inserted before the forbidden class name. And for constraint kind "no_call_to_methods_in" when the scope kind is "methods", the sentence is: Method members that are declared in "<scoped>" should not call Method members that are declared in "<forbidden>".

oracle_data.golden_api_signature: public static string ToErrorMessage(this IEnumerable<EvaluationResult> evaluationResults)

trigger_keywords: how is a dependency violation described
format of offending class violation message
hidden_constraints.oracle_response: For a type dependency violation the line is: <offending full class name> does depend on <forbidden full class name>. Example: SampleApp.Domain.Order does depend on SampleApp.Infrastructure.Database

trigger_keywords: how is a method call violation described
format of caller and callee in violation
hidden_constraints.oracle_response: For a method call violation the line is: <caller full IL signature> does call <callee full IL signature>. A full IL signature has the form: ReturnType DeclaringType::MethodName(ParamType1,ParamType2,...) where all types are fully-qualified. Example: System.Void SampleApp.Services.ReportService::Generate(SampleApp.Data.Repository) does call System.String SampleApp.Data.Repository::Load(System.Int32)

On error the adapter emits "error=<category>\n" where category is one of: invalid_request (malformed JSON or missing required fields), unknown_scope (unsupported scope kind), unknown_constraint (unsupported constraint kind), unknown_type (class name not found in corpus). For unknown_type an additional line "type=<name>\n" is appended immediately after.

trigger_keywords: what values are valid for scope kind
which element types can be scoped
hidden_constraints.oracle_response: The two supported scope kinds are "types" (constrains a set of classes by full name) and "methods" (constrains every method member declared in the listed classes). Any other value for scope.kind causes the adapter to emit error=unknown_scope.

trigger_keywords: corpus types in the sample model
which classes exist in the fixed code model
what types are in the reference assembly
hidden_constraints.oracle_response: The fixed sample corpus contains exactly six types: SampleApp.Infrastructure.Database (has a method taking int returning string, no dependencies), SampleApp.Data.Repository (has method Load(System.Int32)->System.String, no dependencies), SampleApp.Domain.Customer (plain type, no dependencies), SampleApp.Domain.Order (depends on SampleApp.Infrastructure.Database), SampleApp.Services.ReportService (its Generate method calls SampleApp.Data.Repository::Load), SampleApp.Services.AuditService (no calls into corpus methods). Names not in this set trigger error=unknown_type.

trigger_keywords: how should I organize the code
should I split into multiple files
should I separate business logic from IO
should business logic touch stdin stdout
how to separate parsing from logic
hidden_constraints.oracle_response: Scale-driven code organization: match physical structure to domain complexity. For micro-utilities or simple scripts a single well-organized file is fine. For systems with multiple distinct responsibilities (I/O routing, business rules, formatters, etc.) you MUST use a multi-file layout (e.g. src/, tests/) — never a single god file. Do not over-engineer simple problems, but strictly avoid monolithic files for complex domains. Strict separation of concerns is mandatory: core business logic must be completely decoupled from stdin/stdout and JSON parsing. The JSON test cases are a black-box contract for the execution adapter only — NOT the internal data model. Follow SOLID: SRP (separate parsing, routing, core execution, output formatting); OCP (core engine open for extension, closed for modification); DIP (high-level modules depend on abstractions, not I/O details). The public interface must be idiomatic. Errors must be modeled properly (typed exceptions or Result/Monad patterns), not generic faults.

trigger_keywords: how does the test harness call my code
what is the entry point for testing
how does the runner call the program
hidden_constraints.oracle_response: You must implement an execution adapter (dispatcher) that is logically and ideally physically separate from the core domain. It reads a single JSON object from stdin, inspects the 'action' field to route to the correct core function, then prints the result to stdout matching the per-feature output contract. All policy durations in inputs are in seconds; all time points are epoch milliseconds. The adapter must not contain any business logic — every numeric result and boolean decision is delegated to the core domain.

Deliver a bash test harness at rcb_tests/test.sh. It must accept --cases-dir <subdir> (default: test_cases) to point at a directory of *.json case files under rcb_tests/. For each case file it runs every case in the 'cases' array against the dispatcher, writing raw stdout (no PASS/FAIL metadata) to: rcb_tests/stdout/<cases-dir>/{filename_stem}@{case_index_zero_padded_3}.txt Example: rcb_tests/stdout/public_test_cases/feature1_next_retry_at@000.txt Output is namespaced by <cases-dir> so different directories never overwrite each other. A single entry point 'bash rcb_tests/test.sh' runs the full suite with the default dir.

trigger_keywords: can I see the test inputs
hidden_constraints.oracle_response: Yes, public test cases are provided, one file per feature. Ask me for a specific feature by name and I will give you the full test case JSON.

context_pointers.vague_reference: handle these two flavors the same way as the auth module handles both direct and predicate-based lookups
context_pointers.actual_meaning: The two dependency constraint kinds (no_dependency_on_types and no_dependency_on_types_matching) produce the same dependency evaluation result but differ only in rule sentence wording — mirroring the pattern in ArchUnitNET/Fluent/Syntax/Elements/Types/TypeConditionsDefinition.cs where NotDependOnAny and NotDependOnAnyTypesThat.Are both ultimately check the same dependency relation but generate different Description strings via the fluent builder.

oracle_data.fallback_response: That detail is not covered by the documented constraints. Please consult the start.md specification or inspect the dispatcher source code directly for clarification.

feature1_1_forbidden_type_dependency.jso.description: A rule selects one or more named classes from the analysed code model and forbids them from depending, in any way (field types, parameter types, base types, method-body references, etc.), on one or more explicitly named target classes. The engine evaluates the rule against the fixed sample corpus and renders a report. When a selected class does have a forbidden dependency, the report contains the quoted rule sentence followed by a tab-indented violation line of the form '<full name of offending class> does depend on <full name of forbidden class>'. When no selected class has the forbidden dependency, the report is the single line stating all evaluations passed.

cases.expected_output:
"Classes that are "SampleApp.Domain.Order" should not depend on "SampleApp.Infrastructure.Database"" failed:
	SampleApp.Domain.Order does depend on SampleApp.Infrastructure.Database

feature1_2_forbidden_dependency_by_ident.description: A rule selects one or more named classes and forbids them from depending on any class that satisfies an identity predicate (the forbidden set is described by 'types that are <named classes>' rather than by passing the classes directly). Behaviourally this constrains the same dependency relation as the direct-target form, but the rule sentence is phrased in terms of the predicate. The engine evaluates against the fixed sample corpus: a selected class with a forbidden dependency yields the quoted rule sentence and a tab-indented '<offending class> does depend on <forbidden class>' line; otherwise the report states all evaluations passed.

cases.expected_output:
"Classes that are "SampleApp.Domain.Order" should not depend on any types that are "SampleApp.Infrastructure.Database"" failed:
	SampleApp.Domain.Order does depend on SampleApp.Infrastructure.Database

feature2_forbidden_method_call.json.description: A rule selects all method members declared in one or more named classes and forbids them from calling any method member declared in one or more named target classes. The engine evaluates against the fixed sample corpus and renders a report. When a selected method calls a forbidden method, the report contains the quoted rule sentence followed by a tab-indented violation line naming both methods by their full IL-style signatures, of the form '<return-type> <caller-signature> does call <return-type> <callee-signature>'. When no selected method calls any forbidden method, the report is the single line stating all evaluations passed.

cases.expected_output:
"Method members that are declared in "SampleApp.Services.ReportService" should not call Method members that are declared in "SampleApp.Data.Repository"" failed:
	System.Void SampleApp.Services.ReportService::Generate(SampleApp.Data.Repository) does call System.String SampleApp.Data.Repository::Load(System.Int32)
